blocks.text: proclaim the message; be persistent whether the time is favorable or unfavorable; convince, rebuke, and encourage, with the utmost patience in teaching.
2 : A succinct list of the tasks of church leaders.
For the time is coming when people will not put up with sound doctrine, but having itching ears, they will accumulate for themselves teachers to suit their own desires,
and will turn away from listening to the truth and wander away to myths.
3–4 : The false teachers as purveyors of myths (cf. 1 Tim 1.4; 4.7; Titus 1.14).
As for you, always be sober, endure suffering, do the work of an evangelist, carry out your ministry fully.
3.10–4.5: Timothy's charge to steadfastness in his ministry.
As for me, I am already being poured out as a libation, and the time of my departure has come.
I have fought the good fight, I have finished the race, I have kept the faith.
6–7 : Sacrificial (Phil 2.17) and athletic (1 Cor 9.24–27; Phil 2.16; 1 Tim 6.12; Acts 20.24) metaphors applied to Paul's missionary life.
From now on there is reserved for me the crown of righteousness, which the Lord, the righteous judge, will give me on that day, and not only to me but also to all who have longed for his appearing.
4.6–8: Paul's reflections on his impending death.
8 : , the winner's prize, signifying positive judgment from the Lord at his Second Coming (1 Thess 2.19; 1 Cor 9.25; Phil 4.1).
Do your best to come to me soon,
9 : An abrupt shift from the previous indication of Paul's imminent death (also 4.21).
for Demas, in love with this present world, has deserted me and gone to Thessalonica; Crescens has gone to Galatia, Titus to Dalmatia.
Only Luke is with me. Get Mark and bring him with you, for he is useful in my ministry.
10–11 : Paul's abandonment at the end (also 1.15; 4.16).
I have sent Tychicus to Ephesus.
10–12 : , cf. Philem 24 and Col 4.10–14. , mentioned only here. On , see Introduction to Titus.
12 : , see Col 4.7; cf. Eph 6.21–22; Acts 20.4; Titus 3.12.
When you come, bring the cloak that I left with Carpus at Troas, also the books, and above all the parchments.
13 : , ancient Troy, the departure point from Asia to Europe (2 Cor 2.12–13; Acts 16.8–12). , mentioned only here. , a suggestive but unspecific reference to Paul's literary proclivities.
Alexander the coppersmith did me great harm; the Lord will pay him back for his deeds.
14 : , see 1 Tim 1.20n.
You also must beware of him, for he strongly opposed our message.
At my first defense no one came to my support, but all deserted me. May it not be counted against them!
16 : , probably a preliminary hearing (cf. Phil 1.7).
But the Lord stood by me and gave me strength, so that through me the message might be fully proclaimed and all the Gentiles might hear it. So I was rescued from the lion's mouth.
17 : , as he will Timothy (2.1; also 1 Tim 1.12). , Ps 21.22 (LXX); Dan 6.21; 1 Macc 2.60.
The Lord will rescue me from every evil attack and save me for his heavenly kingdom. To him be the glory forever and ever. Amen.
4.8–18: An update from prison.
Greet Prisca and Aquila, and the household of Onesiphorus.
19 : , prominent missionary team and close associates of Paul (Rom 16.3; 1 Cor 16.19; Acts 18.2,18). , 1.16–17.
Erastus remained in Corinth; Trophimus I left ill in Miletus.
20 : , the city-treasurer of Corinth (Rom 16.23; cf. Acts 19.22). , Acts 20.4; 21.29.
Do your best to come before winter. Eubulus sends greetings to you, as do Pudens and Linus and Claudia and all the brothers and sisters.
4.19–21: Epistolary greetings.
21 : All of these people who are with Paul (despite 4.11!) are found only here. , while a sea voyage is possible.
The Lord be with your spirit. Grace be with you.
4.22: Concluding benediction.
